prompt:
Create a therapeutic audio session for the following goal. Write the full script in English, approximately 15 minutes long when read aloud at a calm pace of about 120 words per minute.

CRITICAL: This script will be read aloud by a text-to-speech engine. Write ONLY plain spoken prose. Absolutely NO markdown formatting — no **, ##, *, -, bullet points, numbered lists, headers, bold, or italic syntax. No section labels. Just natural flowing speech.

## Goal
Title: Process grief and keep the memory of grandpa alive
Description: Help Sam understand and express grief after the loss of his grandfather.

## Person
This is for Sam (age 7).
Developmental Tier: MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD

CRITICAL AGE REQUIREMENT: Sam is a 7-year-old child (MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD tier). Every word of this script MUST be written for a child, NOT for an adult.
- Use only simple words (1-2 syllables when possible).
- Use playful, warm, concrete language — no abstract adult concepts.
- NEVER say Sam is "normal like an adult", "behaves like an adult", or describe adult-level coping.
- NEVER use adult register, adult emotional vocabulary, or adult expectations.
- If you find yourself writing for a grown-up, stop and rewrite for a 7-year-old child.

## Research Evidence
The following research papers inform the therapeutic techniques to use:

1. "Child-centered grief therapy for bereaved children 6-10" (2023)
   Key findings: Memory boxes and continuing-bonds activities reduced grief symptoms and improved adjustment
   Therapeutic techniques: Memory box activity; continuing bonds narration; safe-place visualization

2. "Expressive arts in childhood bereavement" (2021)
   Key findings: Drawing and storytelling about the deceased improved emotional processing in 5-10 year olds
   Therapeutic techniques: Memory drawing; story narration; feelings thermometer; love never disappears reframe

## Audio Script Requirements
- Write as spoken prose ONLY — the listener cannot see any text, they can only hear
- Use "..." (three dots) for all pauses — between sections, after instructions, within sentences. NEVER write [pause] or any bracket markers — TTS engines read them literally
- Keep sentences short: maximum 15 words each
- Use spoken transitions: "Now..." "Next..." "When you're ready..." "Good. Let's try..."
- Include breath-timing cues for breathing exercises: "Breathe in... two... three... four..."
- Vary pacing: alternate between instruction, story or metaphor, and silence
- Never give more than two instructions in a row without an ellipsis pause or encouragement
- Incorporate specific techniques and findings from the research above
- When clinical notes are available, weave their insights and observations into the session
- Address Sam by name at least 3 times throughout the session
- Personalize for Sam (age 7) (developmental tier: MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD)
- This is for a 7-year-old child — use child vocabulary, playful framing, and age-appropriate techniques throughout. Never adult-register.
- Target duration: 15 minutes (approximately 1800 words at calm pace)
- Write in English
- Include a brief mention that a parent, caregiver, or professional can provide additional support if needed
script:
Hi Sam... I'm really glad you're here with me today... I know you might be feeling some big feelings... because your grandpa isn't here anymore... That's a really hard thing to feel... And it's okay to feel that way... Lots of kids feel sad and mixed up when someone they love has to go... Today, we're going to do some special things together... We're going to play with memories... and build something for your heart... Just like building with LEGO... We'll go nice and slow... You're doing great just by listening... Ready to start?

First, let's get our bodies comfy... You can sit or lie down... Maybe wiggle your toes to get cozy... Good... Now, put one hand on your tummy... and one hand on your heart... Feel your hands there... They're like two friendly helpers... Let's take a big, slow breath together... Breathe in through your nose... like you're smelling a warm cookie... one... two... three... Good... Now breathe out through your mouth... like you're blowing on that cookie to cool it down... one... two... three... four... Let's do that two more times... Your own special way... In... two... three... And out... two... three... four... One more time... In... two... three... And out... two... three... four... Good job, Sam.

You know, Sam... feelings can be like LEGO bricks... Sometimes we have a red brick feeling... that feels angry or hot... Sometimes a blue brick feeling... that feels quiet and sad... And sometimes a yellow brick... a sunny, happy feeling... When we miss someone very much... we might have lots of blue bricks... and maybe some red ones too... All those bricks are okay... They are all part of your special heart... Today, we're going to build something with those feeling-bricks... We won't push them away... We'll just look at them together... and see what we can make.

I want you to think of your grandpa for a moment... Don't worry about getting it perfect... Just think of his face... Maybe his smile... or his laugh... What's one thing you remember? ... It could be a big thing or a tiny thing... Maybe he made a funny face... or his hands felt warm... Just hold that one memory in your mind... like holding a special LEGO piece...

Now, Sam... I want you to imagine building a box... A memory box... This isn't a sad box... This is a treasure box... For all the good memories of your grandpa... In your mind, what color is this box? ... You get to pick... Is it blue like the sky? ... Or green like grass? ... Pick your favorite color... Good... Now imagine putting that first memory inside... the one you just thought of... You can even pretend to place it with your hands... There it goes... safe in the box...

Let's find some more treasures for your box... Think of a time you felt happy with grandpa... What were you doing? ... Were you playing a game? ... Was he reading you a story? ... Or maybe you were just sitting together being quiet... Find that happy memory... Now, in your mind, pick up that memory... and put it in your special box... You can even nod your head when you put it in... Good.

Sometimes our hearts feel sore when we remember... and that's okay... That sore feeling is because your love for him is so big... Love is like super glue for LEGO bricks... It never really disappears... Even if you can't see the person anymore... the love is still there... sticking everything together inside your heart... Can you feel that? ... The love is still there...

Let's do our breathing again... Hand on your heart... Remember the love glue that's in there... Breathe in... two... three... Imagine breathing in that warm, gluey love... Breathe out... two... three... four... Let the air take any wiggly feelings out with it... Good, Sam.

Now, I have a story about a boy... a builder, just like you... This builder loved his big tree in the backyard... He played under it every day... Then one winter, a big storm came... And the old tree fell down... The builder felt very sad... He missed his climbing tree... He missed its shade... His heart felt full of blue bricks... So he decided to build something... He gathered pieces of the old tree... smooth bark... a twisty twig... He even found an old bird's nest that had fallen... He built a little boat from the wood... And he put the twig as a mast... And the nest became a little cabin... He couldn't play under the tree anymore... But he could sail his new boat... and remember the tree in a new way... The love for the tree was the glue... that held his new boat together.

We can do that with memories too, Sam... We can build something new with them... If you have some paper and crayons near you, you could grab them... If not, that's fine... we can just imagine... I want you to draw a picture of your treasure box... Or draw one of the memories inside it... Don't worry about it being a perfect drawing... Just let your hand move... Maybe you draw grandpa's hat... or his favorite chair... or you both eating ice cream... Just let the picture happen... I'll be quiet while you draw or imagine... ...

... That's wonderful... However you did it, that was just right... You are taking care of your memories, Sam... That is very brave.

Let's put our hands on our hearts again... Feel your heart beating... ba-bump... ba-bump... That's your heart saying hello... It holds all your memories... all your love... Let's send a little thank you to our hearts... Breathe in... two... three... And as you breathe out... whisper or think "thank you"... ...

Sometimes we have questions... or feelings that feel too big for our box... That's when we need to share them... You can always tell a grown-up you trust... You can say, "My heart feels full of blue bricks today"... or "I was thinking about grandpa"... Sharing feelings is like asking a friend to help you build a big LEGO tower... It makes it easier and more fun.

I want you to think of one thing about your grandpa that makes you smile... Just one little thing... Maybe the way he said your name... Or a joke he told... When you have it, give your own arm a little squeeze... That's you giving that memory a hug... Good.

Now, let's build one last thing together... A bridge... A brave bridge from today to tomorrow... Imagine a LEGO bridge... Each step on the bridge is something you can do... The first step is remembering it's okay to feel sad... You're on that step right now... The next step is talking to someone... Another step is drawing a picture... Another is looking at photos... You don't have to run across the bridge all at once, Sam... You can just stand on this first step... and know the other steps are there for later... Your love is the glue that holds this whole bridge together... Strong and steady.

You did such good work today, Sam... You thought about your grandpa... You built a memory box in your heart... You even sent love to your own heart... That is amazing.

Here's something you can try later... With a mom or dad or someone who cares for you... You can ask them to help you make a real memory box... You could put in a drawing... a photo... a rock from a walk you took with grandpa... Anything that feels like a treasure... You can add to it whenever you want... It's your special collection.

Always remember, Sam... Your love for grandpa is like that super glue... It doesn't disappear... And it's okay to miss him... It's okay to feel all the colored bricks... You are brave... You are loved... And your grandpa's memory is safe with you.

Thank you for building with me today... Be extra gentle with yourself... You can come back and listen anytime your heart needs a friend. If those big feelings ever feel too heavy, Sam, always tell a grown-up you trust. They can help you carry them. You're doing great. Goodbye for now.
